incidents.symptoms_observed: P99 latency spiked from 450ms to 9200ms. Error rate increased to 12% (504 timeouts). DB connection pool reached 98% utilization. Alert fired at 14:32.
incidents.root_cause: A deployment 20 minutes prior introduced a missing database index on the transactions table. A new query pattern added in the release performed full table scans under load, exhausting connection pool and causing timeout cascade.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified missing index via EXPLAIN ANALYZE on slow queries. 2. Added index: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_transactions_user_id ON transactions(user_id). 3. Latency normalised within 3 minutes of index creation. 4. Verified no further timeouts.
contributing_factors: Missing DB index
No query performance testing before deployment
Connection pool too small relative to concurrent requests
action_items: Add query performance tests to CI pipeline
Increase DB connection pool to 50
Add index coverage check in pre-deploy hook

incidents.symptoms_observed: Error rate reached 45%. All order creation requests returning 500. Logs showed NullPointerException in payment gateway integration module. Service health endpoint still returned 200.
incidents.root_cause: Deployment introduced a code path that didn't handle the case where the external payment gateway returned a new response field format introduced in a gateway API version update. NullPointerException on response parsing.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Immediately rolled back to v3.8.2 within 8 minutes of detection. 2. Error rate dropped to 0% within 2 minutes of rollback. 3. Root cause identified in code review: missing null check on new gateway response field. 4. Fixed in v3.8.4 and redeployed with smoke test.
contributing_factors: No integration test with latest payment gateway API version
Missing null safety in response parsing
No canary deployment process
action_items: Add payment gateway integration tests against latest API version
Enable canary deployment for order-service
Add null safety linting rules

incidents.symptoms_observed: CPU utilization reached 96% on all api-gateway instances. P99 latency increased from 180ms to 4500ms. No deployment in previous 4 hours. Traffic was 2.3× normal baseline.
incidents.root_cause: Marketing campaign launched at 11:00 AM drove unexpected traffic surge. The api-gateway was configured with a maximum of 2 replicas. Auto-scaling had a 5-minute cooldown period, causing the cluster to remain undersized during the spike.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Manually scaled api-gateway replicas from 2 to 8. 2. CPU dropped from 96% to 28% within 3 minutes. 3. Latency normalised within 5 minutes. 4. Investigated auto-scaling misconfiguration.
contributing_factors: Auto-scaling cooldown too long for sudden traffic spikes
Marketing did not notify engineering before campaign launch
Maximum replica count too low
action_items: Reduce auto-scaling cooldown to 60 seconds
Establish marketing-engineering coordination process for launches
Increase max replica limit for api-gateway to 20

incidents.symptoms_observed: Memory utilization grew from 45% to 98% over 4 hours before OOM kill. Service restarted automatically 3 times. Each restart caused Kafka consumer group rebalance, temporarily increasing lag.
incidents.root_cause: An unbounded in-memory cache was added for email template rendering. The cache had no maximum size or TTL configured. Under normal load, the cache grew to consume all available memory over ~4 hours.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Restarted service manually to restore memory. 2. Identified cache misconfiguration in code review. 3. Added LRU cache with max_size=1000 and TTL=300s. 4. Deployed hotfix. 5. Monitored memory for 2 hours to confirm fix.
contributing_factors: No memory growth alerting at intermediate levels
Cache added without eviction policy
No load testing for extended duration
action_items: Add memory alert at 75%
Code review checklist: all caches must have max_size and TTL
Add 4-hour soak test to CI pipeline

incidents.symptoms_observed: DB connections reached 100/100. All new payment requests failing with 'connection pool exhausted'. Latency spiked to 15000ms before full failure. Revenue impact estimated at $50K/minute.
incidents.root_cause: A batch reconciliation job was deployed to run at 8 PM. It used a separate connection pool but shared the same database server. The batch job opened 80 connections for parallel processing, leaving only 20 for the application. The 20 remaining connections were quickly exhausted under normal transaction load.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Killed the batch reconciliation job to free connections. 2. DB connections immediately dropped to 20. 3. Application recovered within 30 seconds. 4. Rescheduled batch job to 3 AM with connection limit of 5.
contributing_factors: Batch job connection limit not configured
No reservation of connections for application vs. batch
Batch job scheduled during peak traffic hours
action_items: Implement PgBouncer for connection pooling
Add connection quotas per application role
Schedule all batch jobs during off-peak hours (1-4 AM)

incidents.symptoms_observed: Kafka consumer lag grew from 0 to 85,000 messages over 2 hours. Order notifications were delayed by up to 90 minutes. Consumer processing rate dropped from 500 msg/s to 12 msg/s.
incidents.root_cause: The notification-service's SMTP provider experienced degraded performance. Each email took 3-8 seconds instead of 200ms. Since notification-service is a synchronous consumer, slow email sending directly reduced its Kafka consumption rate.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified slow SMTP provider via notification-service logs. 2. Switched to backup SMTP provider. 3. Processing rate recovered to 480 msg/s. 4. Lag consumed in 45 minutes. 5. Added async email queue to decouple SMTP from Kafka consumption.
contributing_factors: Synchronous SMTP in Kafka consumer hot path
No secondary SMTP provider failover
No lag alerting threshold configured
action_items: Decouple email sending from Kafka consumer with internal queue
Configure SMTP provider failover
Add Kafka lag alert at 5000 messages

incidents.symptoms_observed: inventory-service returned 503 for all requests. order-service showed 35% error rate (order validation failures). Root cause was in inventory-service, not order-service.
incidents.root_cause: PostgreSQL experienced a checkpoint storm caused by a large batch import job. The write pressure caused checkpoint duration to spike, and during checkpoint, query latency exceeded the 5-second timeout in inventory-service, which then failed health checks.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Cancelled the batch import job. 2. PostgreSQL checkpoint completed in 4 minutes. 3. inventory-service recovered automatically. 4. order-service error rate dropped to 0%.
contributing_factors: Batch import job scheduled during business hours
PostgreSQL checkpoint_completion_target too aggressive
No circuit breaker in inventory-service for DB timeouts
action_items: Schedule all batch imports during off-peak hours
Tune checkpoint_completion_target to 0.9
Add circuit breaker for DB calls in inventory-service

incidents.symptoms_observed: user-service error rate jumped to 100% immediately after deployment. All authentication requests failing with 500. Other services depending on user-service started showing errors. MTTR was fast due to quick rollback decision.
incidents.root_cause: A Redis connection string format change in the new deployment was incompatible with the existing Redis client library version. All Redis operations failed with 'invalid URI format', causing all user authentication to fail.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Detected 100% error rate within 90 seconds of deployment. 2. Immediately triggered rollback. 3. Rollback completed in 3 minutes. 4. Error rate dropped to 0% within 30 seconds of rollback completing.
contributing_factors: Redis URI format change not tested against actual Redis client version
No smoke test running post-deployment
No canary deployment
action_items: Add post-deployment smoke test that validates Redis connectivity
Use integration tests with actual Redis client library
Enable 5% canary deployment for all user-service releases

incidents.symptoms_observed: payment-service showed intermittent connection resets to Redis. Error rate fluctuated between 0% and 80% in 30-second cycles. Redis health checks passed. Retry storms amplified the load.
incidents.root_cause: A network switch firmware upgrade created intermittent connectivity between the payment-service pods and the Redis cluster. The upgrade was applied during the maintenance window but affected production traffic.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified network switch as root cause via infrastructure monitoring. 2. Rolled back switch firmware. 3. Connectivity stabilised. 4. payment-service error rate returned to baseline.
contributing_factors: Infrastructure change during maintenance window without traffic shifting
Retry logic without exponential backoff amplified errors
No network partition test in staging
action_items: Always shift traffic away from affected zone before infrastructure changes
Implement exponential backoff with jitter for all Redis calls
Add network partition chaos testing quarterly

incidents.symptoms_observed: api-gateway P99 latency grew from 180ms to 3200ms. All downstream services appeared healthy. The latency was concentrated in the gateway itself, not in downstream calls.
incidents.root_cause: A configuration change to the nginx rate limiter introduced a misconfigured shared memory zone that caused mutex contention between worker processes. The contention grew with request rate.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified the configuration change from deployment history. 2. Reverted nginx configuration to previous version. 3. Reloaded nginx (zero-downtime). 4. Latency normalised within 2 minutes.
contributing_factors: Configuration change not load tested before deployment
No automated config validation in CI
Rate limiter shared memory zone undersized
action_items: Add nginx config load test in staging before production deployment
Implement automated nginx config validation
Document rate limiter sizing guidelines

incidents.symptoms_observed: order-service error rate reached 60%. Logs showed errors from payment-service returning unexpected 422 validation errors. No deployment to order-service. payment-service was deployed 25 minutes prior.
incidents.root_cause: payment-service v2.15.0 introduced stricter input validation that rejected a field format that order-service had been sending for 18 months. The payment-service deployment broke the contract between services.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified payment-service deployment as root cause via deployment timeline. 2. Rolled back payment-service to v2.14.3. 3. order-service errors ceased immediately. 4. Implemented proper API versioning.
contributing_factors: No contract testing between services
Breaking API change deployed without coordination
API versioning not implemented
action_items: Implement Pact contract tests for payment-service API
Create cross-service API change review process
Version all APIs to prevent breaking changes

incidents.symptoms_observed: notification-service memory grew from 40% to 99% over 6 hours. Service was OOM killed. 3 restart cycles before issue was identified. Each restart caused Kafka consumer rebalance.
incidents.root_cause: A Kafka consumer message listener was storing references to processed Kafka messages in a list for audit purposes. The list was never cleared. Over 6 hours of processing, the list accumulated 2.4 million message references consuming 2.8GB of memory.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Added memory limit trigger for restart at 85%. 2. Added TTL to audit list (24-hour rolling window). 3. Deployed fix. 4. Memory stabilised at 42%.
contributing_factors: Unbounded audit list without TTL or size limit
Memory alert only at 95% (too late)
No extended soak test in staging
action_items: Alert on memory at 75%
Bound all in-memory data structures with TTL or max size
Add 8-hour soak test to staging pipeline

incidents.symptoms_observed: DB connections reached max. Payment transactions failing. Idle-in-transaction connections were accumulating — 40 of 100 connections were in 'idle in transaction' state for >5 minutes.
incidents.root_cause: An application bug caused database transactions to remain open after the HTTP response was returned to the client. Specifically, the exception handler for 4xx errors returned early without committing or rolling back the open transaction.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Terminated all idle-in-transaction connections older than 2 minutes. 2. Connections freed immediately. 3. Deployed hotfix that ensures all code paths commit or rollback before returning. 4. Added idle_in_transaction_session_timeout=30s to PostgreSQL.
contributing_factors: Transaction lifecycle not guaranteed in all code paths
No idle_in_transaction_session_timeout configured
No monitoring of idle-in-transaction connection count
action_items: Set idle_in_transaction_session_timeout=30000 in PostgreSQL
Add connection state monitoring alert
Code review: all exception handlers must close transactions

incidents.symptoms_observed: api-gateway CPU at 92%. Latency p99 increased to 2800ms. Traffic was only 1.1× normal. No deployment in previous 24 hours.
incidents.root_cause: A logging change deployed 3 days earlier enabled debug-level JSON logging with stack traces for all requests. The JSON serialization of stack traces was extremely CPU-intensive. The issue manifested gradually as log volume grew.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified JSON stack trace serialization as hot path via CPU profiling. 2. Changed log level back to INFO. 3. CPU dropped from 92% to 28% within 1 minute. 4. Disabled debug logging in production permanently.
contributing_factors: Debug logging enabled in production
Logging configuration not load tested
No CPU baseline monitoring to detect gradual growth
action_items: Enforce INFO log level for production deployments in CI
Add CPU growth rate alert
Load test logging configuration changes

incidents.symptoms_observed: inventory-service P99 latency spiked to 12000ms. order-service showed 40% error rate due to inventory check timeouts. DB query times were normal. Network latency between inventory-service and DB was elevated.
incidents.root_cause: A disk saturation event on the PostgreSQL host caused increased I/O wait times. The database was a secondary concern — the primary symptom was network-level slowness caused by the host's I/O scheduler deprioritizing network under disk pressure.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified disk saturation on DB host. 2. Found a log rotation process was compressing large log files, consuming 100% of disk IOPS. 3. Killed the compression process. 4. Disk I/O normalised. 5. DB and inventory-service latency recovered.
contributing_factors: Log rotation scheduled during business hours
Log compression too CPU/IO intensive
No disk I/O monitoring
action_items: Schedule log rotation to off-peak hours
Use streaming compression to limit IOPS impact
Add disk I/O saturation alert at 80%

incidents.symptoms_observed: Error rate reached 75% immediately after deployment. TypeError in order serialization. Health endpoint returned 200 (health check didn't exercise the broken code path).
incidents.root_cause: A type annotation change in the Order model introduced an implicit type conversion that failed for existing order records stored in the database with the old schema. New orders worked fine; reading existing orders failed.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Rolled back within 5 minutes of detection. 2. Errors ceased immediately. 3. Fix: added explicit data migration for the type conversion before redeploying.
contributing_factors: Health check didn't test order retrieval
No staging data migration test
Type annotation change treated as non-breaking
action_items: Expand health check to cover read and write paths
Add staging environment with production data snapshot for pre-deploy testing
Treat all model type changes as potentially breaking

incidents.symptoms_observed: payment-service latency grew from 400ms to 3500ms. Error rate was only 2%. Investigation showed slow Redis cache responses. Redis memory was 95% utilized.
incidents.root_cause: Redis was running with maxmemory-policy=noeviction. As cache filled up, Redis started returning errors for new writes, causing the application to fall through to the database for every request. DB became the bottleneck.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Changed Redis maxmemory-policy to allkeys-lru. 2. Redis immediately began evicting old entries. 3. Application cache hit rate recovered. 4. Latency normalised within 5 minutes.
contributing_factors: Redis eviction policy not configured for production
No Redis memory utilization alert
Application didn't handle Redis write errors gracefully
action_items: Set maxmemory-policy=allkeys-lru in Redis configuration
Add Redis memory alert at 80%
Implement graceful cache-miss handling (fallback to DB without error)

incidents.symptoms_observed: api-gateway returning 502 for all requests. user-service health check failing. All services depending on user-service authentication failing. Root cause was user-service OOM.
incidents.root_cause: user-service experienced a memory leak from an unclosed Redis connection pool. The pool grew until OOM killed the service. Since api-gateway requires authentication (user-service) for all requests, all requests failed.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified user-service OOM from system logs. 2. Restarted user-service. 3. All api-gateway requests recovered. 4. Root cause: connection pool not closed on Redis errors. 5. Fixed connection cleanup.
contributing_factors: Redis connection leak on errors
No circuit breaker for user-service in api-gateway
No authentication fallback/caching
action_items: Always close connections in finally blocks
Add api-gateway circuit breaker for user-service with 5-minute auth cache
Set Redis connection pool max_idle_connections

incidents.symptoms_observed: Order event consumer lag reached 120,000 messages. Notifications were delayed by 3+ hours. Consumer was running but processing at 10% of normal rate.
incidents.root_cause: A regex pattern added to the order event processor for audit logging was not anchored. On certain order descriptions, the regex caused catastrophic backtracking, taking 2-3 seconds per message instead of microseconds.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified slow messages via consumer timing logs. 2. Found catastrophic regex in log analysis. 3. Fixed regex to anchored pattern with timeout. 4. Deployed fix. 5. Consumer rate returned to 500 msg/s. 6. Lag consumed in 4 minutes.
contributing_factors: Regex not tested against adversarial input
No per-message processing time monitoring
Regex without timeout
action_items: Add ReDoS testing to CI for all regex patterns
Add per-message processing time metrics and alert at 500ms
Set regex timeout of 100ms for all pattern matching

incidents.symptoms_observed: P99 latency at 8500ms. DB queries from payment-service were slow. Query analysis showed a specific query doing full table scans. This query was introduced in the latest deployment.
incidents.root_cause: A new payment search feature added a query with ILIKE on an unindexed column. The query performed full table scans on the 50M-row transactions table, taking 8-12 seconds each, and was called on every payment status check.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified slow query via pg_stat_statements. 2. Added partial index: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_transactions_status ON transactions(status) WHERE status != 'completed'. 3. Latency dropped to 350ms immediately after index creation. 4. Also added query result caching with 30s TTL.
contributing_factors: ILIKE query on large unindexed column
No query performance test in CI
Query called on hot path (every payment status check)
action_items: Add EXPLAIN ANALYZE requirement for new queries in code review
Set statement_timeout=5000 to fail fast
Add pg_stat_statements monitoring to CI baseline comparison

incidents.symptoms_observed: user-service P99 latency increased from 220ms to 2100ms. Redis cache hit rate dropped from 72% to 8%. All user authentication requests degraded.
incidents.root_cause: A Redis configuration change accidentally set a 60-second key TTL on all session tokens (previously 24 hours). Sessions expired rapidly, causing every request to miss cache and hit the database.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified low cache hit rate in Redis metrics. 2. Found the TTL misconfiguration from config change history. 3. Reverted Redis configuration. 4. Cache warmed up over 10 minutes. 5. Latency normalised.
contributing_factors: Configuration value in wrong unit (seconds vs. milliseconds)
No config change testing
No cache hit rate monitoring
action_items: Add Redis configuration unit tests
Add cache hit rate alert at < 50%
Review all configuration values in code review with unit annotation

incidents.symptoms_observed: inventory-service error rate reached 100% immediately after deployment. All stock check requests failing. Panic on startup in new code path.
incidents.root_cause: A nil pointer dereference in the new inventory reservation code path caused a panic on the first request that triggered the code path. Go's panic recovery middleware caught it but returned 500 for all subsequent requests.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Immediate rollback within 3 minutes. 2. Error rate dropped to 0% within 1 minute. 3. Root cause: nil check missing for optional configuration field.
contributing_factors: No nil check for optional configuration
New code path not covered by unit tests
No canary deployment
action_items: 100% unit test coverage required for new code paths
Enable canary deployment for inventory-service
Add nil safety lint rules for Go code

incidents.symptoms_observed: CPU at 98%. Latency p99 at 5000ms. Traffic was only 1.0× normal. Profiling showed excessive goroutine creation.
incidents.root_cause: A middleware change created a new goroutine for every request for async logging. Goroutines were not being garbage collected quickly enough, accumulating to 50,000+ goroutines, causing excessive memory pressure and GC pauses.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Rolled back middleware deployment. 2. CPU normalised within 2 minutes. 3. Fixed: used a worker pool instead of unbounded goroutine creation.
contributing_factors: Unbounded goroutine creation
No goroutine count monitoring
Async logging not using pool pattern
action_items: Add goroutine count monitoring and alert at 10,000
Use worker pool pattern for all async operations
Load test middleware changes before production deployment

incidents.symptoms_observed: payment-service error rate at 15%. Errors were connection timeouts to Redis. Redis was healthy. Network diagnostics showed intermittent packet loss between payment-service pods and Redis.
incidents.root_cause: A Kubernetes network policy update inadvertently added a QoS policy that rate-limited traffic between the payment-service namespace and the Redis namespace to 10 Mbps. High-throughput Redis pipelining hit the limit during peak traffic.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified network policy change in Kubernetes audit log. 2. Reverted QoS policy. 3. Packet loss ceased. 4. Error rate dropped to 0% within 30 seconds.
contributing_factors: Network policy not tested with production traffic volumes
QoS limit too low for Redis pipeline throughput
No network bandwidth monitoring between namespaces
action_items: Test all network policy changes with production traffic volumes in staging
Add inter-namespace bandwidth monitoring
Document all network QoS policies with throughput requirements

incidents.symptoms_observed: order-service P99 latency reached 7000ms. Payment service was healthy. DB queries were fast. The delay was in the order creation code path specifically.
incidents.root_cause: A distributed tracing addition made all service calls sequential instead of concurrent. Payment validation, inventory check, and fraud check (previously concurrent) were now called in sequence, tripling the response time.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified sequential calls in distributed trace view. 2. Reverted tracing integration that caused the regression. 3. Latency returned to 380ms. 4. Fixed tracing to preserve concurrent execution.
contributing_factors: Tracing integration broke async pattern
No latency regression test in CI
Code review missed the async→sync change
action_items: Add latency regression test: assert P99 < 500ms for order creation
Review all await/async patterns in code review
Add integration test that validates concurrent downstream calls

incidents.symptoms_observed: notification-service Kafka consumer lag growing. Errors in logs: 'Topic not found: order.events.v2'. The topic was renamed in a Kafka schema migration.
incidents.root_cause: The Kafka topic was renamed from order.events to order.events.v2 as part of a schema migration. notification-service was still configured to consume from the old topic name. The consumer started cleanly but consumed no messages.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified missing topic subscription from consumer group monitoring. 2. Updated notification-service configuration to subscribe to order.events.v2. 3. Deployed update. 4. Consumer started consuming from beginning of new topic. 5. Lag recovered.
contributing_factors: Topic rename not communicated to all consumer teams
No consumer subscription validation on startup
No cross-service change coordination process
action_items: Add consumer subscription validation: fail startup if topic doesn't exist
Implement cross-service change communication process
Use topic aliases instead of renaming to support gradual migration

incidents.symptoms_observed: payment-service CPU at 97%. Latency P99 at 6000ms. Error rate 8%. Thread dump showed most threads blocked in RSA key generation.
incidents.root_cause: A cryptographic change switched from pre-generated RSA keys to on-demand RSA key generation for each transaction. RSA-4096 key generation is extremely CPU-intensive (~200ms per key). Under 50 RPS, this consumed all CPU.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Rolled back crypto change. 2. CPU normalised within 1 minute. 3. Fix: implemented RSA key pool with pre-generation.
contributing_factors: CPU cost of on-demand key generation not evaluated
No load test for cryptographic operations
No CPU profiling in code review for crypto changes
action_items: Load test all cryptographic operations before deployment
Implement key pool with pre-generation for RSA operations
Add CPU profiling step to CI for changes touching crypto modules

incidents.symptoms_observed: user-service error rate at 20%. Errors: 'JWT signature verification failed'. All recently-logged-in users were affected. Previously cached sessions still worked.
incidents.root_cause: A JWT secret key rotation was performed without a grace period. The new key was applied immediately, invalidating all tokens signed with the old key. Users with cached sessions were unaffected; users making new requests had invalid tokens.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Detected JWT errors in logs. 2. Identified key rotation as cause. 3. Added dual-key verification: accept both old and new key for 24-hour transition period. 4. Errors dropped to 0% immediately.
contributing_factors: No graceful key rotation process (dual verification period)
Key rotation process not documented
No impact analysis before rotating credentials
action_items: Implement dual-key JWT verification for all key rotations
Document key rotation procedure with rollout and rollback steps
Notify team 24 hours before any credential rotation

incidents.symptoms_observed: DB connection pool exhausted. All order write operations failing. Read operations unaffected (using separate read pool). Connections were accumulating in 'BEGIN' state.
incidents.root_cause: A new order cancellation feature used nested transactions. When the outer transaction failed, the inner transaction's connection was not properly released back to the pool. Cancellations during error conditions leaked connections.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Terminated orphaned connections. 2. Deployed hotfix with proper savepoint cleanup. 3. Added connection_timeout monitoring.
contributing_factors: Nested transaction cleanup not tested under error conditions
No connection leak monitoring
Complex transaction logic not reviewed for resource cleanup
action_items: Add connection pool monitoring with active/idle breakdown
Require integration tests for all new transaction code paths
Add static analysis for unclosed database transactions

incidents.symptoms_observed: inventory-service P99 latency at 9000ms. DB query times were normal. Network roundtrip from inventory-service to postgres was showing 2-3 second delays.
incidents.root_cause: PostgreSQL statistics were severely out of date. After a large data import, the auto-vacuum and auto-analyze processes had not run. The query planner was using stale statistics and choosing sequential scans over index scans.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Ran ANALYZE on the inventory table manually. 2. Query planner immediately switched to index scans. 3. Latency dropped to 300ms within 1 minute. 4. Configured aggressive autovacuum for inventory table.
contributing_factors: Auto-analyze not configured aggressively enough for bulk imports
No query plan monitoring
Large data import without manual ANALYZE afterward
action_items: Run ANALYZE immediately after all bulk data imports
Configure per-table autovacuum settings for high-write tables
Add query plan change monitoring alert

incidents.symptoms_observed: api-gateway P99 latency at 3000ms. Downstream services appeared healthy. The delay was in the gateway itself on all request types.
incidents.root_cause: A TLS certificate renewal deployed a new certificate with a long OCSP stapling response. On each new TLS connection, the gateway was fetching OCSP responses synchronously, adding 2-3 seconds of latency to connection establishment.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified OCSP stapling as the bottleneck via nginx debug logging. 2. Configured OCSP response caching. 3. Latency normalised within 5 minutes. 4. Also configured OCSP Must-Staple to prevent future issues.
contributing_factors: OCSP stapling not configured
TLS renewal not tested for latency impact
No TLS handshake time monitoring
action_items: Configure OCSP stapling caching with 1-hour TTL
Add TLS handshake time monitoring
Test all certificate renewals in staging for latency impact

incidents.symptoms_observed: payment-service showed 60% error rate during maintenance window. Errors were connection resets to PostgreSQL. DB was healthy. Network diagnostic showed routing issue.
incidents.root_cause: A BGP route update during maintenance window caused a brief asymmetric routing condition. Outbound packets from payment-service pods took one path; return packets from PostgreSQL took a different path that traversed a stateful firewall that had no session state for the connection.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified asymmetric routing via traceroute. 2. Reverted BGP route update. 3. Symmetric routing restored. 4. Connection resets ceased.
contributing_factors: BGP update during maintenance window still affected production
No asymmetric routing validation test
Stateful firewall not configured for asymmetric routing
action_items: Validate routing symmetry after all network changes
Configure stateful firewall for asymmetric routing tolerance
Add network topology change to pre-change checklist

incidents.symptoms_observed: order-service memory growing from 50% to 95% over 3 hours. No obvious memory consumers. Garbage collection logs showed objects surviving multiple GC cycles.
incidents.root_cause: An event listener added for order status change notifications was never removed when the associated HTTP request context completed. Each request created a listener that held a reference to the request context, preventing GC.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Added memory profiling to identify object retention chains. 2. Found orphaned event listeners referencing completed request contexts. 3. Fixed: added cleanup in request context finalizer. 4. Memory stabilised.
contributing_factors: Event listener lifecycle not tied to request lifecycle
No memory profiling in code review for event-heavy features
Missing cleanup in request finalizer
action_items: Audit all event listener registrations for proper cleanup
Add memory profiling test for request handlers
Document event listener lifecycle requirements

incidents.symptoms_observed: user-service CPU at 99%. All authentication requests timing out. Event loop blocking detected. Node.js not processing requests.
incidents.root_cause: A user profile export feature added a synchronous JSON serialization of large profile objects in the request handler. For accounts with thousands of historical records, this blocked the Node.js event loop for 5-30 seconds.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Immediately rolled back profile export feature. 2. CPU normalised within 30 seconds. 3. Fix: moved profile serialization to worker threads.
contributing_factors: Synchronous operation on Node.js main thread
No event loop block time monitoring
Feature not tested with large accounts
action_items: Add event loop lag monitoring, alert at 100ms
Require worker thread usage for all CPU-intensive operations
Load test new features against 99th percentile data sizes

incidents.symptoms_observed: payment-service P99 latency at 4000ms. DB was healthy. External payment gateway was slow. Retry logic was amplifying the latency by retrying on the same slow gateway.
incidents.root_cause: The retry configuration for the external payment gateway was changed to retry 5 times on timeout with no circuit breaker. When the gateway was slow (>2s response time), the retry logic resulted in 10+ second total response times.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified retry amplification in distributed traces. 2. Added circuit breaker: after 3 failures in 10 seconds, fail fast for 60 seconds. 3. Latency dropped to 2200ms (still high due to gateway). 4. Gateway recovered independently.
contributing_factors: Retry configuration without circuit breaker
No total timeout budget for retries
External dependency without fallback
action_items: Add circuit breaker to all external payment gateway calls
Set total timeout budget: max 3s regardless of retry count
Add fallback for payment gateway timeouts (queue for retry)

incidents.symptoms_observed: 100% error rate for all API requests. HTTP 500 with error 'route not found'. All routes suddenly invalid.
incidents.root_cause: A deployment of the routing configuration used an invalid YAML syntax that caused nginx to fail to load routes. nginx fell back to a default config that rejected all requests.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Detected 100% error rate within 60 seconds. 2. nginx log showed 'emerg: invalid YAML'. 3. Rolled back routing config. 4. nginx reloaded and routes restored within 2 minutes.
contributing_factors: YAML syntax not validated before deployment
No nginx config test (nginx -t) in CI
No canary deployment for configuration changes
action_items: Add nginx -t config validation in CI pipeline
YAML syntax validation in pre-commit hook
Route smoke test immediately after configuration deployment

incidents.symptoms_observed: inventory-service DB connections exhausted. Stock check operations failing. Connection pool debug showed many connections in 'waiting for lock' state.
incidents.root_cause: A new bulk inventory update feature held row-level locks for the duration of a multi-step update process (read, calculate, write). Under concurrent flash sale traffic, thousands of requests waited for the same row locks.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified lock contention via pg_blocking_pids query. 2. Cancelled long-running lock holders. 3. Deployed fix: reduced lock hold time by batching the update atomically. 4. Added SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED for non-critical updates.
contributing_factors: Long lock hold duration during multi-step operations
No lock timeout configured
No concurrency testing for bulk operations
action_items: Set lock_timeout=5000ms on all transactions
Use SKIP LOCKED for non-critical inventory updates
Add concurrency test for flash sale simulation

incidents.symptoms_observed: order-service error rate at 70%. Errors: inventory-service returning 503. inventory-service showed CPU at 99%. order-service was the downstream but inventory-service was the root cause.
incidents.root_cause: A load testing job was accidentally run against the production inventory-service by a developer who used the wrong environment variable. The load test generated 10× normal traffic, saturating inventory-service CPU.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified load test traffic source from server logs. 2. Stopped load test. 3. inventory-service CPU normalised within 1 minute. 4. order-service error rate dropped to 0%.
contributing_factors: Load test accidentally ran against production
No production API rate limiting to prevent this
Environment variables not validated against production URLs
action_items: Add production API rate limiting (500 RPS per IP)
Add production URL safeguard in load testing tool
Require peer review for load test environment configuration

incidents.symptoms_observed: Kafka consumer lag for notification-service grew to 200,000 messages. All Kafka partitions showed lag. Consumer was running but processing at near-zero rate.
incidents.root_cause: A Kafka broker disk became 99% full. Kafka started throttling producers and consumers to prevent disk overflow. Consumer throughput dropped to near zero as messages were throttled at the broker level.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified disk usage on Kafka broker. 2. Found old topic data not subject to retention policy. 3. Manually deleted old log segments. 4. Disk dropped to 60%. 5. Kafka throttling removed. 6. Consumer lag recovered.
contributing_factors: No Kafka broker disk alert
Retention policy not set on all topics
No capacity planning for Kafka storage
action_items: Add Kafka broker disk alert at 80%
Enforce retention policy on all topics in topic creation script
Quarterly Kafka capacity planning review

incidents.symptoms_observed: payment-service error rate reached 35%. Errors: 'decimal precision overflow'. Only transactions above $10,000 were failing.
incidents.root_cause: A currency conversion feature introduced a floating-point calculation for large transaction amounts. For amounts above $10,000, cumulative floating-point precision errors caused the final amount to exceed the database column precision (DECIMAL(10,2)), resulting in a database constraint violation.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified pattern: only large transactions failing. 2. Found floating-point precision issue in code. 3. Deployed hotfix using Python's Decimal type for currency calculations. 4. Verified no floating-point arithmetic in any payment code.
contributing_factors: Float used for currency arithmetic
Test cases only covered small amounts
No static analysis for float-based currency operations
action_items: Mandate Decimal type for all currency operations (linting rule)
Add test cases for amounts at 1K, 10K, 100K, 1M
Add DB precision overflow handling in application layer

incidents.symptoms_observed: api-gateway P99 latency at 2500ms. Health checks passing. Downstream services healthy. Latency concentrated in header parsing.
incidents.root_cause: A security feature added custom JWT header validation using a regex that had O(n²) complexity for certain malformed tokens. Attackers (or malformed clients) sending crafted headers caused severe processing delays.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified header parsing as bottleneck via profiling. 2. Replaced regex with linear-time parser. 3. Latency normalised immediately. 4. Added 1ms timeout for header validation.
contributing_factors: Polynomial regex used for header validation
No complexity analysis of security code
No header validation timeout
action_items: All regex in hot paths must pass complexity review
Add 1ms timeout for all header validation operations
Automated ReDoS vulnerability scanning in CI

incidents.symptoms_observed: order-service P99 latency at 5000ms. Only GET /orders/history requests were slow. Other endpoints healthy. DB query showed N+1 pattern.
incidents.root_cause: A new order history feature loaded the order list and then for each order made a separate database query to load the associated payment details. For users with 50+ orders, this was 51 database queries instead of 1.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified N+1 query pattern in slow query log. 2. Rewrote query with JOIN. 3. Order history endpoint dropped from 5000ms to 180ms. 4. Deployed fix.
contributing_factors: N+1 query introduced by ORM lazy loading
No query count monitoring in development
No latency test for history endpoint with 50+ orders
action_items: Add query count logging in development mode (N+1 detection)
Add latency test for order history with realistic data volumes
ORM code review checklist: check for lazy loading in loops

incidents.symptoms_observed: inventory-service returning 422 validation errors for valid requests. order-service showing 40% error rate. No deployment to inventory-service. order-service was deployed 1 hour prior.
incidents.root_cause: order-service v4.7.0 changed the format of product IDs from string UUIDs to integer IDs. inventory-service expected UUID format and rejected the new format. The API contract was broken.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified format mismatch from error logs. 2. Rolled back order-service to v4.6.1. 3. Error rate dropped to 0%. 4. Coordinated both services to deploy together in v4.7.1.
contributing_factors: API contract changed without coordinating with consumers
No contract tests between services
Breaking change treated as minor version bump
action_items: Implement Pact contract tests for inventory-service API
Require cross-team API change review
Treat all ID format changes as major breaking changes

incidents.symptoms_observed: user-service memory growing steadily. After 6 hours, OOM killed the service. Redis connection count also growing, suggesting both memory and connection leak.
incidents.root_cause: A user analytics feature was creating a new Redis client per request for analytics tracking, rather than reusing the shared connection pool. Each request leaked a Redis connection and its associated in-process memory.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified Redis client creation pattern in code review. 2. Deployed fix: shared Redis client for analytics. 3. Memory and Redis connection count normalised.
contributing_factors: New Redis client created per request
No connection count monitoring per Redis client
Code review missed the pattern
action_items: Enforce singleton pattern for Redis client in code review
Add Redis client count monitoring
Add connection pool usage to health endpoint metrics

incidents.symptoms_observed: payment-service P99 latency at 7000ms. All queries slow. DB CPU at 15% (not saturated). Disk I/O at 95%.
incidents.root_cause: PostgreSQL WAL (Write-Ahead Log) archiving fell behind. The WAL directory grew to fill the disk partition. PostgreSQL was forced to wait for archiving before writing new WAL, creating I/O contention on every write operation.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified WAL archiving lag. 2. Suspended WAL archiving temporarily. 3. Cleared disk space. 4. Resumed WAL archiving. 5. Payment service latency normalised.
contributing_factors: WAL archiving falling behind without alerting
WAL directory on same partition as data
No disk space alert for WAL partition
action_items: Move WAL directory to separate partition
Add WAL archiving lag alert
Add disk space alert at 70% for all DB partitions

incidents.symptoms_observed: order-service CPU at 95%. Latency P99 at 4500ms. Traffic was only 1.05× normal. Thread profiling showed time in JSON deserialization.
incidents.root_cause: A webhook receiver for order status updates from external partners was accepting payloads up to 10MB. A partner started sending malformed, deeply nested JSON that caused O(n²) deserialization time. A single 500KB malformed payload consumed 30 seconds of CPU.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Added payload size limit (50KB max). 2. Added JSON depth limit (50 levels max). 3. Deployed. 4. CPU normalised immediately.
contributing_factors: No input size limits on webhook receiver
No JSON depth validation
External partner not rate limited
action_items: Add 50KB payload size limit to all webhook endpoints
Add JSON depth limit of 50 levels
Rate limit external partner webhooks to 100 RPS

incidents.symptoms_observed: api-gateway CPU at 88%. Latency P99 at 2000ms. Traffic was 1.5× normal due to a scheduled marketing email blast that drove inbound traffic.
incidents.root_cause: The horizontal pod autoscaler had a 3-minute cooldown on scale-up. The traffic increase from the email blast was rapid (30 seconds), so the autoscaler could not react fast enough. Manual scaling resolved the issue.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Manually scaled api-gateway to 12 replicas. 2. CPU dropped to 35%. 3. Latency normalised. 4. Reduced autoscaler cooldown to 30 seconds.
contributing_factors: Auto-scale cooldown too long for sudden spikes
Marketing team did not give advance notice
No pre-scaling for known traffic events
action_items: Reduce HPA scale-up cooldown to 30 seconds
Establish marketing notification SOP for campaigns expected to drive >2× traffic
Pre-scale for all scheduled marketing campaigns

incidents.symptoms_observed: payment-service error rate at 20%. Errors: 'TLS handshake failed'. All HTTPS connections to external payment processors failing.
incidents.root_cause: An SSL certificate bundle update removed an intermediate CA certificate that was required by the external payment processor. The payment processor's certificate chain was now unverifiable.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified TLS error from SSL handshake debugging. 2. Found missing intermediate CA in certificate bundle. 3. Re-added intermediate CA. 4. Restarted payment-service. 5. TLS handshakes succeeded.
contributing_factors: SSL bundle update not tested against all external endpoints
No TLS connectivity test in CI
Intermediate CA removal not flagged as breaking change
action_items: Add TLS connectivity test to all external payment processors in CI
Treat CA bundle changes as breaking changes requiring careful review
Document which CAs are required by which external services

incidents.symptoms_observed: inventory-service returning 503. Healthy internally. Cannot connect to PostgreSQL. postgres health endpoint returning 200. Network connection between pods failing.
incidents.root_cause: A Kubernetes pod security policy update restricted egress from the inventory namespace. The new policy blocked connections to the database port (5432) from inventory pods.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified network policy as root cause via kubectl describe pod. 2. Added egress rule for port 5432 to postgres namespace. 3. Connectivity restored. 4. inventory-service recovered.
contributing_factors: Pod security policy update not tested against all egress requirements
No egress connectivity smoke test in CI
Network policy change applied to production directly
action_items: Test all pod security policy changes in staging first
Add database connectivity test to all services' startup health checks
Document all required egress rules for each service

incidents.symptoms_observed: order-service P99 latency at 6000ms. All order processing slow. Deployment was made 15 minutes prior. DB metrics normal. The delay was in the application code itself.
incidents.root_cause: A deployment added response body compression using gzip for all API responses. For small responses (<1KB), the gzip compression overhead exceeded the transfer size savings, adding 50-100ms per request. For bulk operations returning many orders, the compression processing was blocking.
incidents.resolution_steps: 1. Identified gzip middleware as the cause via profiling. 2. Configured compression threshold: only compress responses >1KB. 3. Latency returned to 370ms. 4. Deployed configuration fix.
contributing_factors: Compression applied without size threshold
Compression overhead not measured
Not tested against bulk endpoints
action_items: Set compression threshold to 1KB minimum
Benchmark compression middleware before enabling for all routes
Test all middleware changes against bulk and single-item endpoints
